Source(s): DBT Skills Manual (Linehan, 2015); Ma et al. (2017) Front Psychol; Cedars-Sinai pediatric guidance
Target Emotional State(s): Acute panic; Diffuse anxiety; Anger/rage; Sleep difficulty; School-related stress
Brief Mechanism: Slow exhalation activates vagus nerve → parasympathetic shift; extended exhale reduces heart rate and cortisol
Czech Adaptation Notes: Nepanikař app includes guided breathing modules; Nevypusť duši recommends dechová cvičení

Source(s): PMC review (2025) on DB in pediatric population ages 6–18; Kelty Mental Health; NCCIH
Target Emotional State(s): Acute panic; Diffuse anxiety; Anger/rage; Rumination
Brief Mechanism: Diaphragmatic expansion stimulates vagal afferents → lowers sympathetic arousal; engages interoception
Czech Adaptation Notes: 92% of pediatric participants in DB programs reported significant relaxation benefit

Source(s): Jacobson (1938); Toussaint et al. (2021) ECAM; JPMR study ages 8–17 (2025 MDPI Medicina)
Target Emotional State(s): Diffuse anxiety; Anger/rage; Sleep difficulty; School-related stress
Brief Mechanism: Tense-release cycle creates proprioceptive contrast → muscles relax below baseline; reduces cortisol; Cohen's d = 1.12 in pediatric RCT
Czech Adaptation Notes: Jacobsonova progresivní relaxace referenced in Nevypusť duši materials

Source(s): Harvard Health (2020) Exercising to relax; DBT TIPP skill (Intense exercise); Linka bezpečí #zvladni20 techniques
Target Emotional State(s): Anger/rage; Acute panic; Urge to self-harm; Sadness/numbness
Brief Mechanism: Intense exercise metabolizes stress hormones (adrenaline, cortisol); endorphin release; redirects proprioceptive focus from emotional to physical sensation
Czech Adaptation Notes: Linka bezpečí: 'Pouštím si hrozně nahlas hudbu a zběsile tancuju' — referenced in their diversion techniques

Source(s): Artigas (1997); Jarero & Artigas (2024) EMDR; 12 peer-reviewed studies on BH effectiveness; EMDR-IGTP protocols
Target Emotional State(s): Acute panic; Dissociation/feeling unreal; Sadness/numbness; Urge to self-harm
Brief Mechanism: Bilateral stimulation engages both hemispheres; loads working memory → reduces vividness/emotional charge of distressing material; activates parasympathetic NS
Czech Adaptation Notes: Developed for trauma survivors; widely used in humanitarian contexts with children; adaptable to Czech setting

Source(s): Somatic Experiencing (Levine, 1997); trauma-informed body-based practices; Kelty Mental Health
Target Emotional State(s): Anger/rage; Dissociation/feeling unreal; Acute panic
Setting Constraints: Needs some privacy or accepting environment
Brief Mechanism: Somatic discharge of stored stress energy through involuntary-mimicking voluntary tremor; resets autonomic nervous system

Source(s): DBT adapted distress tolerance; occupational therapy proprioceptive input literature
Target Emotional State(s): Anger/rage; Acute panic; Urge to self-harm; School-related stress
Brief Mechanism: Isometric muscle contraction provides heavy proprioceptive input → calming; redirects fight impulse into controlled physical effort without harm

Source(s): Hagen & Nayar (2014) yoga for pediatric anxiety; multiple RCTs reviewed in IntechOpen (2025)
Target Emotional State(s): Diffuse anxiety; Sadness/numbness; Sleep difficulty; School-related stress
Brief Mechanism: Combines proprioceptive input, breath regulation, and interoceptive focus; activates parasympathetic NS; improves body awareness

Source(s): CBT behavioral activation; mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR); GoodRx Health pediatric guidance
Target Emotional State(s): Rumination/thought spirals; Sadness/numbness; Diffuse anxiety; Loneliness
Brief Mechanism: Bilateral rhythmic movement; attention shift to body sensations and environment; gentle cardiovascular activation lowers cortisol
Bridges to Help-Seeking: Yes (walking to a place where people are)

Source(s): Polyvagal theory (Porges); somatic trauma practices; clinical recommendation
Target Emotional State(s): Acute panic; Dissociation/feeling unreal; Loneliness; Sadness/numbness
Brief Mechanism: Firm pressure on torso activates deep touch pressure receptors → releases oxytocin; provides containment signal to nervous system

Source(s): Vagal tone research (Porges); bee breath (Bhramari) in yoga literature; clinical consensus
Target Emotional State(s): Acute panic; Diffuse anxiety; Dissociation/feeling unreal
Brief Mechanism: Humming vibrates laryngeal muscles adjacent to vagus nerve → direct vagal stimulation; extended exhalation effect; auditory self-soothing

Source(s): Somatic Experiencing; trauma-informed grounding; occupational therapy
Target Emotional State(s): Dissociation/feeling unreal; Anger/rage; Acute panic
Setting Constraints: Anywhere (can be discreet — pressing feet firmly)
Brief Mechanism: Strong proprioceptive input through feet reconnects to body; bilateral stimulation; channels anger energy downward

Source(s): Grounding techniques in CBT/DBT; RCT in children with Tourette (36-point anxiety reduction); Lang et al. (2022) JBCT
Target Emotional State(s): Acute panic; Dissociation/feeling unreal; Diffuse anxiety; Rumination
Brief Mechanism: Engages prefrontal cortex through structured sensory attention → interrupts amygdala-driven anxiety loop; reorients to present moment
Czech Adaptation Notes: Referenced across Czech mental health materials; Nepanikař includes grounding modules

Source(s): DBT Self-Soothe skill (smell); olfactory-limbic connection literature; clinical recommendation
Target Emotional State(s): Acute panic; Diffuse anxiety; Sadness/numbness; Dissociation
Required Objects: Scented object (soap, essential oil, spice, favorite cream, tea bag)
Brief Mechanism: Olfactory nerve has direct pathway to amygdala and hippocampus → bypasses cognitive processing; can trigger positive memory associations instantly

Source(s): DBT Self-Soothe (touch); sensory integration therapy; occupational therapy literature
Target Emotional State(s): Dissociation/feeling unreal; Diffuse anxiety; Rumination
Required Objects: Any textured objects nearby (fabric, stone, keys, bark)
Brief Mechanism: Tactile sensory input grounds attention in the body and present moment; activates somatosensory cortex → competes with rumination circuits

Source(s): DBT distress tolerance (taste self-soothe); clinical consensus for urge redirection
Target Emotional State(s): Dissociation/feeling unreal; Urge to self-harm; Sadness/numbness
Required Objects: Sour candy, lemon, strong mint, spicy gum
Brief Mechanism: Intense gustatory stimulation provides immediate sensory interrupt; activates trigeminal nerve → shifts attention from emotional to physical sensation without causing harm
Czech Adaptation Notes: Referenced in Nevypusť duši self-harm alternatives

Source(s): DBT TIPP (Temperature — adapted: mild, not shock); vagal response literature
Target Emotional State(s): Acute panic; Anger/rage; Urge to self-harm
Required Objects: Access to cold water (sink, water bottle)
Brief Mechanism: Mild cold on wrists/cheeks activates mammalian dive reflex → rapid parasympathetic shift; reduces heart rate. NOTE: gentle application only, not shock/immersion
Czech Adaptation Notes: Adapted: excludes ice cubes on skin / cold immersion per safety constraints

Source(s): Music therapy meta-analyses; Thoma et al. (2013) cortisol study; GoodRx Health pediatric guidance
Target Emotional State(s): Sadness/numbness; Diffuse anxiety; Anger/rage; Loneliness; Sleep difficulty
Brief Mechanism: Music modulates serotonin, dopamine; 60–80 BPM tempo entrains heartbeat toward rest; lyrics provide cognitive engagement and emotional validation
Czech Adaptation Notes: Linka bezpečí references loud music + dance as diversion technique in #zvladni20

Source(s): Deep Pressure Stimulation literature; Grandin (1992); occupational therapy
Target Emotional State(s): Acute panic; Diffuse anxiety; Sleep difficulty; Dissociation
Required Objects: Heavy blanket, large pillow, or rolled towel
Brief Mechanism: Deep pressure touch activates parasympathetic NS; releases serotonin and dopamine; reduces cortisol; mimics swaddling containment

Source(s): Sensory processing literature; ADHD accommodations research; occupational therapy consensus
Target Emotional State(s): Diffuse anxiety; Rumination; School-related stress
Required Objects: Fidget spinner, stress ball, putty, smooth stone, keychain
Brief Mechanism: Repetitive tactile input provides low-level sensory regulation; occupies motor cortex → reduces anxious restlessness; somatosensory grounding

Source(s): Art therapy meta-analysis (PMC 2024); Malchiodi (2012) art therapy with children; Tartakovsky (2015)
Target Emotional State(s): Sadness/numbness; Anger/rage; Diffuse anxiety; Shame; Rumination
Brief Mechanism: Externalization of internal emotional state through visual metaphor; engages right hemisphere; provides symbolic distance from overwhelming feelings; activates creative flow state

Source(s): Pennebaker (1997) expressive writing; CBT thought records; Fukumori et al. (2017) structured writing and self-regulation
Target Emotional State(s): Rumination/thought spirals; Sadness/numbness; Anger/rage; Shame; Loneliness
Required Objects: Paper + pen, or phone notes app
Brief Mechanism: Expressive writing externalizes cognitive loops → reduces working memory load on rumination; emotional labeling (affect labeling) reduces amygdala activation
Bridges to Help-Seeking: Yes (written material lowers threshold for sharing)
Czech Adaptation Notes: Nepanikař app includes 'Moje záznamy' journaling module

Source(s): Narrative therapy; Gestalt empty chair technique (adapted); clinical consensus
Target Emotional State(s): Anger/rage; Shame; Loneliness; Sadness/numbness; Rumination
Brief Mechanism: Externalizes interpersonal conflict or unexpressed emotion; cognitive defusion from relationship distress; creates sense of agency without confrontation risk
Bridges to Help-Seeking: Yes (practices articulating feelings → bridge to real conversation)
Czech Adaptation Notes: Linka bezpečí suggests written expression as alternative to direct confrontation

Source(s): Art therapy for anxiety (meta-analysis PMC 2024); Curry & Kasser (2005) mandala coloring and anxiety; Zentangle method
Target Emotional State(s): Diffuse anxiety; Rumination; School-related stress; Sadness/numbness
Brief Mechanism: Structured repetitive pattern creation induces flow state; anchors attention in present motor task; reduces default mode network rumination

Source(s): Music therapy literature; adolescent identity and music research; clinical recommendation
Target Emotional State(s): Sadness/numbness; Anger/rage; Loneliness; Diffuse anxiety
Brief Mechanism: Active selection engages executive function and identity expression; combines cognitive reappraisal (choosing songs that match/shift mood) with musical soothing

Source(s): Expressive arts therapy; anger management clinical recommendations; Linka bezpečí diversion techniques
Target Emotional State(s): Anger/rage; Urge to self-harm; Rumination
Required Objects: Old magazines, newspapers, scrap paper; optional glue + new paper
Brief Mechanism: Physical tearing provides proprioceptive release for anger energy; constructive destruction satisfies action urge without harm; creative reconstruction adds agency
Czech Adaptation Notes: Linka bezpečí lists trhání papíru as diversion technique; Nevypusť duši references similar

Source(s): Narrative therapy; self-compassion research (Neff, 2003); clinical recommendation
Target Emotional State(s): Loneliness; Sadness/numbness; Rumination; Shame
Brief Mechanism: Hearing own voice externalizes internal dialogue; creates observer perspective (cognitive defusion); records can be replayed for self-validation
Bridges to Help-Seeking: Yes (practices verbalizing feelings → lowers barrier to calling helpline)
Czech Adaptation Notes: Bridge toward calling Linka bezpečí 116 111

Source(s): Music therapy; hip-hop therapy (Tyson, 2002); adolescent creative expression literature
Target Emotional State(s): Anger/rage; Sadness/numbness; Loneliness; Shame; Rumination
Brief Mechanism: Structures emotional expression within creative constraint (rhythm, rhyme); engages executive function; provides mastery experience and identity expression
Bridges to Help-Seeking: Yes (can share lyrics as indirect disclosure)

Source(s): Art therapy (Malchiodi, 2012); sensory integration; expressive arts therapy
Target Emotional State(s): Anger/rage; Diffuse anxiety; Urge to self-harm; Rumination
Required Objects: Modelling clay, playdough, or DIY salt dough
Brief Mechanism: Combines tactile sensory input with creative expression; squeezing/shaping provides proprioceptive outlet for anger; three-dimensional externalization of feelings

Source(s): Art therapy body mapping; somatic approaches; clinical recommendation for children
Target Emotional State(s): Dissociation/feeling unreal; Diffuse anxiety; Sadness/numbness; Anger/rage
Brief Mechanism: Drawing emotions as located in the body reconnects somatic and emotional awareness; builds interoceptive vocabulary; counters dissociation through embodied attention
Bridges to Help-Seeking: Yes (map becomes conversation tool with trusted adult)

Source(s): ACT (Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, Hayes et al.); cognitive defusion; DBT mindfulness 'observe' skill
Target Emotional State(s): Rumination/thought spirals; Diffuse anxiety; Shame; School-related stress
Brief Mechanism: Cognitive defusion: adding 'I notice I'm having the thought…' prefix creates psychological distance between self and thought content → reduces fusion with negative cognition
Czech Adaptation Notes: ACT widely used in Czech clinical psychology

Source(s): EMDR safe/calm place protocol; guided imagery RCTs; IntechOpen (2025) pediatric stress review
Target Emotional State(s): Acute panic; Diffuse anxiety; Sleep difficulty; Dissociation; Loneliness
Setting Constraints: Quiet space preferred; possible anywhere with eyes closed
Brief Mechanism: Guided imagery activates same neural circuits as real sensory experience; creates competing positive emotional state; reduces amygdala activation through positive memory/imagination
Czech Adaptation Notes: EMDR-IGTP protocols widely used internationally; guided imagery in Czech child psychology practice

Source(s): CBT stimulus control for worry (Borkovec, 1983); clinical recommendation for GAD
Target Emotional State(s): Rumination/thought spirals; Diffuse anxiety; School-related stress
Required Objects: Nothing (optional: paper to note worry for later)
Brief Mechanism: Scheduled worry window breaks reinforcement cycle of immediate rumination; trains cognitive flexibility; most worries feel less urgent when revisited later

Source(s): CBT cognitive restructuring; self-compassion (Neff, 2003); Nevypusť duši recommendation
Target Emotional State(s): Shame; Rumination; School-related stress; Sadness/numbness
Brief Mechanism: Perspective-taking exercise breaks self-critical loop; activating compassionate self-talk engages affiliative soothing system → counteracts threat system dominance
Bridges to Help-Seeking: Yes (practices compassionate language usable with others)
Czech Adaptation Notes: Nevypusť duši: 'mluvit sám k sobě soucitně, jako byste mluvili k nejlepšímu příteli'

Source(s): DBT Crisis Survival (Linehan, 2015); widely used in adolescent DBT programs
Target Emotional State(s): Acute panic; Anger/rage; Urge to self-harm
Brief Mechanism: S-T-O-P: Stop, Take a step back, Observe, Proceed mindfully. Creates pause between trigger and reaction → engages prefrontal cortex before impulsive response

Source(s): Marlatt (1980s); DBT distress tolerance (Linehan); Therapist Aid handout; widely used in DBT-A
Target Emotional State(s): Urge to self-harm; Anger/rage; Rumination
Brief Mechanism: Mindful observation of urge as wave → acceptance rather than suppression; urge intensity peaks and naturally subsides; reduces reactivity through non-judgmental awareness
Czech Adaptation Notes: Linka bezpečí #zvladni20 aligns with this: urge peaks last ~20 min

Source(s): DBT distraction (ACCEPTS — Activities); clinical consensus; dissociation management
Target Emotional State(s): Acute panic; Dissociation/feeling unreal; Rumination
Brief Mechanism: Counting backwards from 100 by 7s (or simpler for younger) engages prefrontal cortex → competes with emotional processing; cognitive load displaces rumination; reorients to present
Czech Adaptation Notes: Nepanikař includes 'Počítání' module

Source(s): DBT Pros and Cons (Linehan, 2015); adolescent CBT protocols
Target Emotional State(s): Urge to self-harm; Anger/rage (before impulsive action)
Brief Mechanism: Structured evaluation of short-term vs long-term consequences engages rational mind; prepares commitment to use skills; can be prepared in advance and carried as reminder card

Source(s): Transitional object theory (Winnicott); narrative therapy; clinical recommendation for younger children
Target Emotional State(s): Loneliness; Sadness/numbness; Shame; Diffuse anxiety
Required Objects: Stuffed animal, pillow, pet (if available), or any object
Brief Mechanism: Externalization through dialogue with object; practices articulating feelings aloud; transitional object provides sense of connection and safety; reduces isolation feeling
Bridges to Help-Seeking: Yes (rehearses verbalization → lowers barrier to telling real person)
Czech Adaptation Notes: Bridge to calling Linka bezpečí 116 111

Source(s): Behavioral rehearsal in CBT; social skills training; clinical consensus
Target Emotional State(s): Shame; School-related stress; Loneliness; Diffuse anxiety
Brief Mechanism: Behavioral rehearsal reduces anticipatory anxiety about real disclosure; builds self-efficacy for help-seeking; desensitizes through repeated exposure to feared conversation
Czech Adaptation Notes: Explicitly recommended as bridge to calling Linka bezpečí or talking to školní psycholog

Source(s): Narrative therapy; positive psychology interventions; self-compassion literature
Target Emotional State(s): Sadness/numbness; Loneliness; Rumination; Shame
Brief Mechanism: Temporal self-distancing (writing to future self) creates hope and perspective; self-compassionate framing; can be reread as anchor during future distress
Repeatability: Moderate (one letter per episode; can vary prompts)

Source(s): Seligman et al. (2005) positive psychology RCT; Emmons & McCullough (2003); Nepanikař 'Co mě potěšilo'
Target Emotional State(s): Sadness/numbness; Rumination; Loneliness; Sleep difficulty
Brief Mechanism: Shifts attentional bias from threat/negative to positive; savoring activates reward circuitry; trains positive memory retrieval; counters negativity bias
Czech Adaptation Notes: Nepanikař includes 'Co mě potěšilo' module

Source(s): Self-compassion (Neff, 2003); CFT (Gilbert, 2009); Nevypusť duši recommendation
Target Emotional State(s): Shame; Sadness/numbness; Loneliness; Urge to self-harm; Rumination
Brief Mechanism: Activates affiliative/soothing system → counteracts threat-based self-criticism; reduces cortisol; builds self-compassion neural pathways through repetition
Czech Adaptation Notes: Nevypusť duši: 'Mrzí mě, že se takhle cítíš a mám tě moc rád'

Source(s): Linka bezpečí chat service; crisis text line research; WHO guidelines on youth mental health services
Target Emotional State(s): Any emotional state; Urge to self-harm; Loneliness; Acute panic
Brief Mechanism: Text-based communication lowers barrier vs voice call; anonymity reduces shame; professional guidance in real time; bridges fully to professional help
Czech Adaptation Notes: Linka bezpečí chat: chat.linkabezpeci.cz; also e-mail pomoc@linkabezpeci.cz; free, anonymous, for children/youth up to 26

Source(s): Earthing/grounding research (Chevalier et al., 2012); somatic grounding practices
Target Emotional State(s): Dissociation/feeling unreal; Diffuse anxiety; Rumination; Anger/rage
Brief Mechanism: Direct tactile contact with earth surface; strong sensory grounding through soles of feet; some evidence for cortisol reduction through electron transfer; primarily sensory reorientation
Czech Adaptation Notes: Accessible in Czech context — parks, gardens, countryside

Source(s): Attention Restoration Theory (Kaplan, 1995); nature-based therapy; adapted 5-4-3-2-1 for outdoors
Target Emotional State(s): Diffuse anxiety; Rumination; Sadness/numbness; School-related stress
Brief Mechanism: Combines sensory grounding with soft fascination of natural environment; attention restoration through involuntary attention → allows directed attention to rest and recover
Czech Adaptation Notes: Czech forests, parks, rivers provide rich sensory environments

Source(s): Mindfulness-based practices; Attention Restoration Theory; clinical recommendation
Target Emotional State(s): Rumination; Diffuse anxiety; Sadness/numbness; Loneliness
Brief Mechanism: Expansive visual field reduces threat-focused tunnel vision; sky provides non-judgmental vast space for projection; soft fascination engages default mode network constructively

Source(s): ACT cognitive defusion techniques (Hayes); humor in therapeutic contexts; clinical recommendation
Target Emotional State(s): Rumination; Shame; School-related stress; Diffuse anxiety
Brief Mechanism: Repeating anxious thought in silly voice/cartoon accent creates cognitive defusion → reduces believability and emotional charge of the thought; humor activates reward circuitry

Source(s): Positive psychology; humor as coping (Martin, 2007); distraction in DBT ACCEPTS
Target Emotional State(s): Sadness/numbness; Loneliness; Diffuse anxiety; Rumination
Brief Mechanism: Laughter triggers endorphin release; shifts facial musculature → afferent signal to brain for positive affect; cognitive distraction from negative loops

Source(s): Safety planning protocols; DBT crisis survival kit; clinical recommendation
Target Emotional State(s): Preventive/all states (prepared in advance for crisis use)
Duration of Activity: 30–60 min to assemble; 1–5 min to use
Required Objects: Box or bag + chosen items (see kit contents list)
Brief Mechanism: Preparation itself builds self-efficacy and agency; pre-selecting items reduces decision fatigue during crisis; tangible kit serves as concrete reminder that coping is possible
Bridges to Help-Seeking: Yes (can include helpline numbers in kit)
Czech Adaptation Notes: Can include Linka bezpečí card (116 111), Nepanikař app reminder, favorite scent, fidget, written affirmations

Source(s): Sleep hygiene literature; CBT-I adapted for adolescents; clinical consensus
Target Emotional State(s): Sleep difficulty; Diffuse anxiety; Rumination
Brief Mechanism: Consistent pre-sleep ritual signals circadian system; reduces sleep-onset anxiety through predictability; combines multiple low-activation techniques (breathing, journaling, sensory soothing)

Source(s): Mindfulness-based practices; positive psychology; clinical recommendation
Target Emotional State(s): Diffuse anxiety; Rumination; Sadness/numbness; School-related stress
Brief Mechanism: Three questions: How am I feeling? What do I need? What's one thing I can do for myself today? — builds interoceptive awareness and self-agency; daily practice strengthens emotional vocabulary
Bridges to Help-Seeking: Yes (noticing patterns → easier to articulate to someone)

Target Emotional State(s): Anger/rage, rigid defiance, urge to self-harm
Brief Mechanism: Utilizes the facial feedback hypothesis; relaxing facial muscles and opening hands biologically contradicts the physiology of anger.

Target Emotional State(s): Diffuse anxiety, thought spirals
Brief Mechanism: Shifting auditory focus from loud to quiet noises requires sustained attentional control, which disrupts catastrophic thought spirals.

Target Emotional State(s): Sadness, numbness, loneliness
Brief Mechanism: Radiant warmth on hands and sweet liquids mimic evolutionary markers of safety and maternal buffering, triggering dopamine.

Target Emotional State(s): School-related stress, acute panic
Brief Mechanism: Tracing the physical outline of a hand forces eyes into deliberate, slow paths, synchronizing breath with visual tracking.

Target Emotional State(s): Anger/rage, urge to self-harm, frustration
Brief Mechanism: Rapid, aggressive motor movements on paper safely discharge kinetic energy associated with the fight response without bodily harm.

Target Emotional State(s): Rumination, diffuse anxiety, school stress
Brief Mechanism: Precise, repetitive fine-motor movements demand concentration, drawing cognitive resources away from anxiety while providing mastery.

Target Emotional State(s): Anger/rage, dissociation
Brief Mechanism: Rhythmic, bilateral tapping bypasses higher cognitive functioning to establish a predictable physical tempo that counteracts internal chaos.

Target Emotional State(s): Anger, sadness, frustration over unchangeable events
Brief Mechanism: Ceasing to fight reality halts the secondary suffering caused by psychological resistance, reducing escalation of the emotional response.

Target Emotional State(s): Overwhelm, sensory overload indoors
Brief Mechanism: The human visual system is optimized for natural fractals; processing these patterns significantly reduces physiological stress and strain.

Target Emotional State(s): Chronic stress, school anxiety, sadness
Brief Mechanism: Sustained immersion in a natural environment significantly lowers cortisol levels, facilitating deep emotional processing away from pressures.

Target Emotional State(s): Diffuse anxiety, sadness, rumination
Brief Mechanism: Rapidly introduces positive affective stimuli, triggering dopamine release and short-circuiting neural pathways processing sadness.

Target Emotional State(s): Shame, social anxiety, fear of failure
Brief Mechanism: Mentally visualizing a feared scenario with an absurd filter structurally defuses the threat response associated with the memory.

Target Emotional State(s): Catastrophizing, thought spirals
Brief Mechanism: Intentionally exaggerating an anxious thought until it becomes logically impossible exposes irrationality, inducing relief through paradox.

Target Emotional State(s): Physical tension, sadness
Brief Mechanism: Forced laughter alters respiratory patterns, extending exhalations to expel tension, eventually triggering genuine endorphin release.

Target Emotional State(s): School stress, environmental transitions
Brief Mechanism: A strict three-step sequence (e.g., wash hands, breathe, touch object) signals the brain that a stressful phase has ended and safety has begun.
